questions.q: A 2-link planar arm has link lengths L1=1m and L2=0.8m. What is the maximum reach from the base?
questions.explanation: Maximum reach equals the sum of all link lengths: 1.0 + 0.8 = 1.8m. This occurs when all joints are fully extended in line. The minimum reach would be their difference (0.2m), and intermediate values depend on joint angles.

questions.q: What does the Denavit-Hartenberg (DH) convention describe?
options: Motor torque ratings
Coordinate frames and transformations between robot joints
Sensor calibration procedures
Joint velocity profiles
questions.explanation: DH convention is a systematic method for assigning coordinate frames to each joint using 4 parameters (a, α, d, θ). It simplifies the representation of forward kinematics equations. Motor torque and calibration are unrelated to DH notation.

questions.q: A 6-DOF industrial robot has all rotational joints. How many degrees of freedom does it have for end-effector positioning and orientation?
options: 3 translational only
4 translational and 2 rotational
6 total (3 translational, 3 rotational)
9 total (all joint angles counted separately)
questions.explanation: Six rotational joints provide 6 total degrees of freedom in 3D space: 3 for position (x, y, z) and 3 for orientation (roll, pitch, yaw). This allows full 6-DOF positioning and orientation of the end effector. Misconception: counting each joint angle separately gives 9, but DOF refers to independent task-space dimensions.

questions.q: In forward kinematics, if you know all joint angles, what can you determine?
options: Only the end-effector position, not orientation
Only the end-effector orientation, not position
The end-effector position and orientation in space
Whether the arm can reach a target location
questions.explanation: Forward kinematics directly computes the position and orientation of the end effector from known joint angles using transformation matrices. Determining reachability (inverse problem) requires solving inverse kinematics and checking constraints.

questions.q: What is a singular configuration in robot kinematics?
options: When two or more joint axes become parallel or aligned
When the end-effector moves outside the workspace
When joint velocities exceed motor limits
When the robot arm hits a self-collision
questions.explanation: A singularity occurs when the Jacobian becomes rank-deficient, typically when joints align (parallel axes) or reach certain geometric configurations. At singularities, the robot loses one or more DOFs and may require infinite joint velocities. Other options describe different problems unrelated to singularities.

questions.q: For a 3-DOF arm with joint angles θ₁, θ₂, θ₃, how many possible inverse kinematics solutions typically exist?
options: Exactly one
Up to 8 solutions depending on arm geometry
Infinitely many
No solutions (IK is unsolvable)
questions.explanation: For a 3-DOF arm with 3-DOF end-effector positioning (x, y, z), inverse kinematics is fully determined but typically has multiple solutions (often 2-8) due to different elbow/shoulder configurations. These are called elbow-up/down, left/right configurations. IK always has at least one solution for reachable points.

questions.q: What is the workspace of a robot?
options: The set of joint angles it can physically reach
The region in space where the end-effector can be positioned
The torque output of each actuator
The set of sensors attached to the arm
questions.explanation: Workspace is the 3D volume (or subset of 3D space) that the robot's end-effector can reach through any combination of joint angles. It's a fundamental property of the robot's geometry and defines its operational region. Joint angles (configuration space) are different from the workspace (task space).

questions.q: How does adding a prismatic (sliding) joint differ from adding a revolute (rotating) joint in terms of DOF contribution?
options: Prismatic joints add 2 DOFs, revolute joints add 1 DOF
Both add 1 DOF to the mechanism
Prismatic joints don't add DOF, only revolute joints do
It depends on the mounting angle
questions.explanation: Both prismatic and revolute joints are lower pairs that contribute exactly 1 DOF each. Prismatic joints provide translation along an axis, while revolute joints provide rotation about an axis. Both independently increase the total DOF by 1.

questions.q: A planar 3-link arm (all revolute joints) is tasked to position its end-effector at point (2, 1). Why might this task be impossible?
options: The point is outside the workspace of the arm
The arm has too few DOFs for the task
Inverse kinematics equations have no real solution
Joint friction prevents reaching that position
questions.explanation: If the total link length is less than the distance from the base to (2, 1), the point lies outside the workspace. For example, links of 1m + 0.8m + 0.6m = 2.4m max reach cannot reach (2, 1) if distance √(4+1) = 2.236m exceeds reachability. Joint friction is a dynamic issue, not a kinematic impossibility.

questions.q: What is the Jacobian matrix in robot kinematics?
options: A matrix relating joint velocities to end-effector velocities
A matrix that stores all joint angles of the robot
A transformation matrix between consecutive robot links
A matrix used to calibrate robot encoders
questions.explanation: The Jacobian (J) relates joint velocities (dθ/dt) to end-effector velocities and twists: v = J·dθ/dt. It's essential for velocity control and inverse kinematics near singularities. Transformation matrices (DH) are different; encoder calibration uses other methods.
